summary: Joel Spolsky addresses the common question of whether employers own software created by programmers in their spare time. He notes widespread misinformation on the topic and frames employment at a tech company as a decision to sell one's intellectual output. The post is essentially a brief pointer to his longer answer on answers.onstartups.com, where he elaborates on intellectual property rights for employee side projects.
key_points: There is significant misinformation about employer ownership of side projects created on personal time
Working at a tech company whose product is intellectual property means you've chosen to sell your intellectual output
The full answer is hosted on answers.onstartups.com rather than elaborated in the blog post itself
money_quotes: There's a surprising amount of misinformation out there about whether software companies own the work that a programmer does in their spare time.
Being an employee of a high tech company whose product is intellectual means that you have decided that you want to sell your intellectual output.
key_insight: Programmers at tech companies should understand that employment fundamentally involves selling their intellectual output, which has implications for side project ownership that are widely misunderstood.
